Hey everybody, before I introduce this section of the Storybrand Marketing Roadmap, I want you to tell me we're having a little switcheroo and now we're wording things. We used to call the Storybrand Brands Script a one page. There was about three months where we tested calling it a one page and then we actually did one of our workshops. We said, hey, do you guys like one page or brand script better? And everybody went brand script, don't call it a one page. Unfortunately, we shot the course during the three months that we were calling it a one page. Whenever we say one page, just think brand script. It's called a brand script in the book. It's called a brand script everywhere else. You can create your brand script at mystorybrand.com. You can create a brand script there. But if you will just forgive us, when I call it a one page, it's actually a brand script and hopefully you can figure that out. It's not too difficult. But if you'll give us that grace, that would be awesome. However, the most important thing is not what you call it, it's that you do it. To actually clarify your message using our brand script methodology. It is going to make a world of difference. So with that, let's get started. Core discipline number one is to create and understand a story brand one page. This is an incredible document that's going to help you simplify and clarify a message for yourself or for your client. We want to create a story brand one page because it's going to simplify everything else we do from here on out. If you are helping a client with their marketing and they understand story brand and you don't help them create a one page, they'll probably think you don't know what you're doing because everything starts with this. We use story to clarify our message because story is the most powerful tool in the world to compel a human brain. Story is a filter that allows us to simplify our message so that people see us, hear us, and understand. The best definition I've ever heard for story is that it is a sense making device. We're going to get to the one page in a second, but right now I just want to back up and I want to explain to you how story works. Here are the seven core elements of a story. Now you're going to see this in almost every movie you see. You see these seven elements. No matter what movie you're watching, a romantic comedy, an action thriller, these seven elements are almost always in play. Every story starts with a character and the most important question we ask when the character comes on is what does the character want? The screenwriter or storyteller has to define exactly what that character wants because it posits a story question. Will the character get what they want? And when we've defined something that the character wants, we finally have a story that gets started. We'll hang around for 90 minutes to see how this thing is going to work out. What this means for us as we're filling out a Story Brand One page is we want to ask ourselves what does the customer want? And here's the problems that we're going to get into. Your client or your boss or even you are going to think, well, my customer wants a lot of things. Well, if we had a story that was about Jason Bourne and Jason Bourne wanted to know who he really was, you know, he's got amnesia. And he also wanted to lose 30 pounds. And he also wanted to adopt a cat. He wanted to marry a girl and he wanted to run a marathon. We would lose the entire audience. Why? Because we've included too many things that Jason wants. There are too many story questions. So the fight for us as brand strategists and as copywriters is just to choose one thing that the customer wants. Now, you can choose one thing for each piece of marketing collateral or for each division of the company. But when you're filling out a Story Brand One page, there's only one answer to the question. One thing that your customer wants and that's how you're going to get a clear and concise story. The second thing that we want to include in our brand script is the problem. And there are three levels of problems in a story. The external problem, the internal problem, and the philosophical problem. Now the best way to remember this is what is the physical problem that the hero is having to deal with? How is that making the hero feel and why is that wrong? That's how you remember the external, internal, and philosophical problem. That's how story works. We want to see all three of those problems resolved preferably in one shot at the end of the movie. What this does for our customer as it relates to branding is it opens up a story loop. When we offer to solve the external problem, say if you have a leaky pipe and you need a plumber, that's the external problem. Internally, people are always frustrated because plumbers are never on time and they charge too much and they make a mess and dealing with plumbers is really frustrating. And philosophically, it's just wrong that somebody would come into your home and not respect and honor your home and all the hard work you've done to create that home. That would be the external, internal, and philosophical problem of a brand, let's say, if you were a plumber. When you feature the resolution to an external problem in your marketing, you do pretty good. When you feature the resolution to an internal problem, for instance, our plumbers are on time and they respect your home and your time or something like this, you see an uptick in engagement. And when you see the philosophical problem featured, that our plumbers come in and they fix your pipes, they are always on time and respect your home because your time is incredibly valuable and you shouldn't waste it waiting on a plumber. You are offering to resolve an external, internal, and philosophical problem when somebody does business with you. The reason the external, internal, and philosophical problem are in almost every story is because for 2,000 years it has worked best to captivate the brain. When for your brand, you feature an external, internal, and philosophical problem and you offer to resolve that when somebody does business with you. You're tapping into 2,000 years of best practices in storytelling and you're going to see an uptick in engagement. I'll talk a little bit more about this later. Then now that our customer has a problem or now that our hero has a problem, we bring in another character into the story and this character is the guide. The guide helps the hero win the day. In Star Wars, this would be Yoda or Obi-Wan Kenobi. In the movie King's Speech, this would be Lionel, the drama coach. The drama coach steps in to help the king overcome his stutter and give a speech that will at the end of the movie resolve the external, internal, and philosophical problem. That's what we're looking for. The biggest paradigm shift for story brand clients and the one that you will have to understand as a story brand copywriter is that the brand should not play the hero in the story. The brand should be the guide. The customer is the hero in the story. The company is the guide. When you position your customer as the guide, you see an uptick in engagement. Why? Because human beings are looking for a guide to help them solve their problems. They're not looking for another hero. In other words, as story brand copywriters, we're really not helping people get their story out. We're helping people fit into their customer's story and we have to understand our customer's story and help the brand fit into that story in order to connect and engage with customers. We want to position ourself as the guide. The two ways we do this are to express empathy. We have to express a sense that we really care about the hero. I feel your pain and I understand your problem. That would be frustrating for me too. That's frustrating for a lot of our clients. The customer needs to hear this statement of you saying, I get it. I understand your pain. They also need authority. The guide needs to express a sense of competence that they can help you solve the problem. For our brands, we want that compassionate statement of I feel your pain. I understand how frustrating it might be to struggle with X and Y, but they also need competence. We've helped over 2,000 other clients resolve this exact problem. That one-two punch of empathy and authority needs to be in the story brand one page because it's going to establish your client or your company in the customer's subconscious as their guide. Remember, a guide is who they're looking for. First we need a plan. There are a couple kinds of plans in your workbook. The main plans are the process plan and the agreement plan. A process plan is best understood as just three or four easy steps somebody can take in order to do business with you. Let's say your client wants to sell used cars. You've started working for a used car a lot and you're helping them create some marketing collateral. You would just want to walk them through three steps that a client could take in order to do business with them. One, come search our inventory of cars. Two, take a test drive. Three, let us get you the best deal possible. It all sounds pretty simple except what happens is customers come to this point where they want to make a purchase and it's scary. This is the first time in the process of a relationship with a customer where they're going to have to take a risk. They could lose money. They could lose time. They could look like a fool. That fog is going to keep them from doing business with whoever it is you're creating a story brand one page. These steps are like stones in a creek that are going to get them across that creek so they will do business with your client or with your company. You want to break things down into three easy steps. The other kind of plan is an agreement plan. An agreement plan is just a list of agreements that your client or your company makes with the customer that eases the tension and fear that they would have of doing business with you. Let's go back to that used car dealer. He's afraid they're going to get ripped off and all that kind of stuff. A brand that I'll show you a little bit more in one of the other modules uses a plan to ease concerns for their customers. A brand called CarMax, a franchise of used car dealers all over America. They do about $8.75 billion a year. They're by far the industry leader. They've built that company by easing customers' concerns about buying used cars or having to wrestle with a used car dealer. They make an agreement plan and that's what lifts the fog and eases the tension. The agreement plan includes things like the price of the car is what you'll pay for the car. We don't haggle. Our salespeople are not paid on commission. There's a 25-point checklist that if any of these things are wrong with this car, we just won't sell it because we never want you to get a lemon. Each of these points in an agreement plan really helps ease the concern of the potential customer so they're more likely to do business with you or your client. We want to understand how these plans work and be able to workshop these plans with clients or within our own company in order to create these plans and put them to use in selling to our customers.
